items.body: Explain "TF-IDF" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: TF-IDF says a term matters more when it appears often in a document (TF) but is rare across the corpus (IDF)

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Calculate a BM25 score by hand
Explain back
items.body: Explain "BM25" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: BM25 fixes TF-IDF's two flaws: term frequency saturates (the 10th occurrence adds little via the k1 parameter) and document length is normalized (the b parameter)

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Stanford IR Book — Evaluation in IR
Compute precision and recall by hand
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Ranking Metrics" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Precision/recall measure set quality; MRR rewards getting one right answer near the top; nDCG rewards graded relevance discounted by rank

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Search Evals" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: You cannot improve retrieval you cannot measure

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Hybrid Search" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Lexical search nails exact terms and rare tokens; vector search captures meaning

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Reranking" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Retrieval is recall-oriented and cheap; reranking is precision-oriented and expensive

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Query Rewriting" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Query rewriting cleans up what the user typed before you look it up in the index: fix spelling, expand synonyms, drop stop words, classify intent

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

Cover: Retries fix transient failures; backoff with jitter stops a retry storm; a dead-letter queue catches messages that will never succeed so they stop blocking the queue and become debuggable

items.title: End-to-End Arguments in System Design (Saltzer et al.)

Explain back

items.title: Kafka: a Distributed Messaging System (Kreps et al.)
Explain back

items.title: Dapper, a Large-Scale Distributed Systems Tracing Infrastructure
Explain back

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "API Design" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: An API is a contract you cannot easily change

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "B-Tree" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A B-tree is a wide, shallow tree sized to disk pages: each node holds many keys so the tree stays ~3-4 levels deep even for billions of rows

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "LSM Tree" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: An LSM tree turns random writes into sequential ones: buffer writes in an in-memory memtable, flush to immutable sorted files (SSTables), and merge them in the background

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Write-Ahead Log" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: The WAL is the database's promise: write the change to an append-only log and fsync it before touching the real data structure

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Compaction" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: An LSM tree keeps producing sorted files; without compaction reads get slower and deleted data lingers

items.title: The Google File System (Ghemawat et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Object Storage" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Object storage (S3/R2) is infinite, cheap, durable bytes — but with ~tens-of-ms latency and no in-place edits

items.title: C-Store: A Column-oriented DBMS (Stonebraker et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Columnar Storage" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A column store groups data by column instead of by row

items.title: Introduction to Information Retrieval (Manning et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Secondary Indexes" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A secondary index is an extra sorted lookup that maps a non-primary-key value (like email) back to the rows that match

items.title: ARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method (Mohan et al.)

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Sharding" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Sharding splits one dataset across many machines so it scales past a single box

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Replication" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Replication keeps copies of data on multiple nodes for durability and read scaling

items.title: Brewer's conjecture and the feasibility of CAP (Gilbert & Lynch)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "CAP & Consistency Models" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: During a network partition you must choose: stay Consistent (reject requests that cannot be confirmed) or stay Available (serve possibly-stale data)

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Object Modeling" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Object modeling is finding the small set of "things" the system has (usually 4-7) and the actions that happen between them

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Strategy Pattern" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: The strategy pattern lets you swap an algorithm at runtime by hiding it behind a common interface

items.title: Applications Programming in Smalltalk-80 (Krasner et al.)

Explain back
items.body: Explain "Factory & Creational" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Creational patterns (Factory, Builder, Singleton) hide how an object is constructed so callers do not pin themselves to a specific concrete class

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Decorator & Structural" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: The decorator pattern wraps an object in another object with the same interface, adding behavior without subclassing

items.title: The Java Memory Model (Manson et al.)

items.body: Explain "Concurrency Design" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Concurrency bugs are usually not "add more locks

items.title: Explain back

items.title: F1: A Distributed SQL Database (Shute et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Booking & Inventory" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Booking systems are about "holds" — a reservation is itself a write that blocks other people

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Game/Simulation Design" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Realtime game servers run a tight loop over a shared world state

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Load Balancing" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A load balancer is the seam between "one address" and "many servers"

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Consistent Hashing" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Consistent hashing maps both servers and keys onto a ring so that when you add or remove a server, only about 1/N of the keys move (instead of nearly all of them)

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

items.title: TAO: Facebook's Distributed Data Store (Bronson et al.)

items.body: Explain "Feed Systems" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Feed systems pick between two strategies: precompute each follower's feed when you post (fan-out on write), or assemble it at request time (fan-out on read)

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Explain back

items.title: Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System (Chang et al.)

Cover: Most graph problems are one of a few templates: reachability/components (DFS/BFS), shortest path (BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra for weighted), ordering (topological sort), or connectivity (union-find)

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Shortest Path" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Pick the algorithm by the graph: BFS for unweighted, Dijkstra when weights are non-negative, Bellman-Ford if weights can be negative, A* when you have a heuristic estimating distance to the goal

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Union-Find" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Union-Find tracks which items are in the same group with two operations: union(a, b) merges two groups, find(a) returns the group id

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "1D DP" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Dynamic programming is recursion with the repeated work cached

items.title: Minimum path sum in a grid
Explain back
items.body: Explain "2D DP" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: 2D dynamic programming fills a table where each cell depends on a few neighbors (often the ones above and to the left)

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Greedy" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A greedy algorithm always picks the choice that looks best right now

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Intervals" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Interval problems almost always start by sorting — usually by start time, sometimes by end

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Math & Geometry" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Interviews use math sparingly: gcd/lcm, modular arithmetic, primes, combinatorics, and basic geometry (cross product, line intersection)

items.title: Graph-based algorithms for Boolean function manipulation (Bryant)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Bit Manipulation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Bits give you three superpowers: a bitmask is an O(1) set, XOR cancels duplicates, and checking power-of-2 is a single AND

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Leadership" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Leadership without authority is getting people aligned on an outcome when you cannot order them to do it

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Conflict Resolution" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Conflict means information surfaced — two people disagree about something that was probably already there

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Decision Making" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Decisions are bets you make with incomplete information

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Collaboration" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Collaboration means making the other person's job easier than they were expecting

items.title: Learning from failures in organizations (Cannon & Edmondson)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Failure & Learning" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A real failure cost something you cannot get back

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Communication" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Good communication is audience-first: after listening, the other person's next decision should be easier

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Prioritization" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Prioritization is saying no to good things, not bad ones

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Innovation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Most real innovation comes from refusing to accept a tradeoff that everyone else is taking for granted

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Customer Obsession" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Customer obsession is not saying yes to whatever they ask

items.title: On the criteria for decomposing systems into modules (Parnas)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Ownership" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Ownership means closing the loop, not playing the hero

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Positioning" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Positioning answers two questions: "for whom" and "compared to what alternative

items.title: Peeking at A/B Tests (Johari et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Landing Pages" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A landing page has one job: get the right visitor to take one action

items.title: The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine (Brin & Page)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "SEO" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: SEO is matching what people are searching for and earning the trust to rank for it through good content and credible links

items.title: Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments (Kohavi et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Product Analytics" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Good analytics looks like a small tree: one north-star metric that captures the value users get, two or three input metrics that drive it, and one guardrail you must not regress on

items.title: Compute a dot product and angle
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Vectors & Vector Spaces" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A vector is a point and an arrow

items.title: Finding Structure with Randomness (Halko et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Matrices & Linear Transformations" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A matrix is a machine that takes vectors in and pushes vectors out

items.title: A Tutorial on Spectral Clustering (von Luxburg)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Eigenvalues & Matrix Decomposition" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Most matrices scramble space, but eigenvectors are the special directions that only get stretched, not rotated

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Probability Fundamentals" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Probability is a bookkeeping system for uncertainty

items.title: Expectation and variance of a discrete RV
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Random Variables & Distributions" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A random variable is a function from outcomes to numbers

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Descriptive Statistics" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Descriptive stats compress a dataset into a few numbers you can reason about

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Estimation & Confidence Intervals" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: A confidence interval is a procedure, not a probability statement about one interval

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Hypothesis Testing" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Hypothesis testing asks: 'how surprising is this data if nothing real changed

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Linear Regression" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Linear regression finds the best linear predictor — geometrically, it projects the target vector onto the column space of your features

items.title: An overview of gradient descent algorithms (Ruder)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Derivatives & Gradients" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: The gradient points uphill steepest

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Multivariable Optimization" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Convex functions have one bowl-shaped minimum — gradient descent cannot get stuck

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Information & Entropy" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Entropy measures average surprise of a distribution

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Classical Distributions" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Distributions are reusable uncertainty templates

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Sampling & the Central Limit Theorem" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Your data is one draw from a bigger game

items.title: Design an A/B test with power
Explain back
items.body: Explain "A/B Testing for Engineers" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: An A/B test is a hypothesis test with a deployment budget

items.title: Bayesian Data Analysis overview (Gelman et al.)
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Bayesian Inference" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Frequentist stats ask how surprising the data is under a null

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Maximum Likelihood Estimation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: MLE picks the parameter values that maximize the probability of what you actually saw

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Covariance & Correlation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Covariance is signed co-fluctuation with units

items.title: Diagnose overfit with a train/holdout split
Explain back
items.body: Explain "Bias, Variance & Overfitting" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Bias is systematic error from too-simple a model; variance is sensitivity to which sample you drew

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Rank, Basis & Subspaces" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Rank counts independent directions the matrix reaches

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "PCA & Projection" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: PCA finds an orthogonal basis ordered by variance

items.title: Deflated Sharpe Ratio (Bailey & López de Prado)

items.body: Explain "Returns & Volatility" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Prices drift; returns are closer to stable

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Stationarity & Autocorrelation" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Stationarity: distribution doesn't drift

items.title: Explain back
items.body: Explain "Random Walks & Markov Chains" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Markov: future depends only on present state

items.title: Explain back

items.body: Explain "Momentum Backtest Discipline" in three sentences a junior engineer would understand — no jargon you can't define. Cover: Backtest = hypothesis on history
